data.title: Is PepGen (NASDAQ:PEPG) In A Good Position To Deliver On Growth Plans?
data.description:
Just because a business does not make any money, does not mean that the stock will go down. For example, although software-as-a-service business Salesforce.com lost money for years while it grew recurring revenue, if you held shares since 2005, you'd have done very well indeed. But while the successes are well known, investors should not ignore the very many unprofitable companies that simply burn through all their cash and collapse.

So should PepGen (NASDAQ:PEPG) shareholders be worried about its cash burn? In this article, we define cash burn as its annual (negative) free cash flow, which is the amount of money a company spends each year to fund its growth. Let's start with an examination of the business' cash, relative to its cash burn.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Does PepGen Have A Long Cash Runway?

You can calculate a company's cash runway by dividing the amount of cash it has by the rate at which it is spending that cash. As at September 2025, PepGen had cash of US$164m and no debt. Importantly, its cash burn was US$86m over the trailing twelve months. So it had a cash runway of approximately 23 months from September 2025. While that cash runway isn't too concerning, sensible holders would be peering into the distance, and considering what happens if the company runs out of cash. You can see how its cash balance has changed over time in the image below.

View our latest analysis for PepGen

How Is PepGen's Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

PepGen didn't record any revenue over the last year, indicating that it's an early stage company still developing its business. So while we can't look to sales to understand growth, we can look at how the cash burn is changing to understand how expenditure is trending over time. Over the last year its cash burn actually increased by 3.6%, which suggests that management are increasing investment in future growth, but not too quickly. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but investors should be mindful of the fact that will shorten the cash runway. Clearly, however, the crucial factor is whether the company will grow its business going forward. For that reason, it makes a lot of sense to take a look at our analyst forecasts for the company.

How Easily Can PepGen Raise Cash?

Since its cash burn is increasing (albeit only slightly), PepGen shareholders should still be mindful of the possibility it will require more cash in the future. Issuing new shares, or taking on debt, are the most common ways for a listed company to raise more money for its business. One of the main advantages held by publicly listed companies is that they can sell shares to investors to raise cash and fund growth. We can compare a company's cash burn to its market capitalisation to get a sense for how many new shares a company would have to issue to fund one year's operations.

PepGen's cash burn of US$86m is about 23% of its US$368m market capitalisation. That's not insignificant, and if the company had to sell enough shares to fund another year's growth at the current share price, you'd likely witness fairly costly dilution.

Is PepGen's Cash Burn A Worry?

On this analysis of PepGen's cash burn, we think its cash runway was reassuring, while its cash burn relative to its market cap has us a bit worried. While we're the kind of investors who are always a bit concerned about the risks involved with cash burning companies, the metrics we have discussed in this article leave us relatively comfortable about PepGen's situation. Taking a deeper dive, we've spotted 4 warning signs for PepGen you should be aware of, and 3 of them make us uncomfortable.

data.title: Private companies in Sanford Limited (NZSE:SAN) are its biggest bettors, and their bets paid off as stock gained 10% last week
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • The considerable ownership by private companies in Sanford indicates that they collectively have a greater say in management and business strategy
  • A total of 6 investors have a majority stake in the company with 51% ownership
  • Institutions own 14% of Sanford

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

A look at the shareholders of Sanford Limited (NZSE:SAN) can tell us which group is most powerful. And the group that holds the biggest piece of the pie are private companies with 37% ownership. That is, the group stands to benefit the most if the stock rises (or lose the most if there is a downturn).

As a result, private companies were the biggest beneficiaries of last week’s 10% gain.

In the chart below, we zoom in on the different ownership groups of Sanford.

View our latest analysis for Sanford

What Does The Institutional Ownership Tell Us About Sanford?

Institutions typically measure themselves against a benchmark when reporting to their own investors, so they often become more enthusiastic about a stock once it's included in a major index. We would expect most companies to have some institutions on the register, especially if they are growing.

Sanford already has institutions on the share registry. Indeed, they own a respectable stake in the company. This suggests some credibility amongst professional investors. But we can't rely on that fact alone since institutions make bad investments sometimes, just like everyone does. It is not uncommon to see a big share price drop if two large institutional investors try to sell out of a stock at the same time. So it is worth checking the past earnings trajectory of Sanford, (below). Of course, keep in mind that there are other factors to consider, too.

Sanford is not owned by hedge funds. The company's largest shareholder is Ngai Tahu Holdings Corporation Limited, with ownership of 20%. With 9.6% and 7.6% of the shares outstanding respectively, Past Limited Partnership and Peter Masfen are the second and third largest shareholders.

We also observed that the top 6 shareholders account for more than half of the share register, with a few smaller shareholders to balance the interests of the larger ones to a certain extent.

Researching institutional ownership is a good way to gauge and filter a stock's expected performance. The same can be achieved by studying analyst sentiments. While there is some analyst coverage, the company is probably not widely covered. So it could gain more attention, down the track.

Insider Ownership Of Sanford

The definition of an insider can differ slightly between different countries, but members of the board of directors always count. Management ultimately answers to the board. However, it is not uncommon for managers to be executive board members, especially if they are a founder or the CEO.

I generally consider insider ownership to be a good thing. However, on some occasions it makes it more difficult for other shareholders to hold the board accountable for decisions.

Our most recent data indicates that insiders own some shares in Sanford Limited. It has a market capitalization of just NZ$604m, and insiders have NZ$46m worth of shares, in their own names. This shows at least some alignment. You can click here to see if those insiders have been buying or selling.

General Public Ownership

With a 37% ownership, the general public, mostly comprising of individual investors, have some degree of sway over Sanford. While this group can't necessarily call the shots, it can certainly have a real influence on how the company is run.

Private Company Ownership

It seems that Private Companies own 37%, of the Sanford stock. It might be worth looking deeper into this. If related parties, such as insiders, have an interest in one of these private companies, that should be disclosed in the annual report. Private companies may also have a strategic interest in the company.

Public Company Ownership

Public companies currently own 4.8% of Sanford stock. It's hard to say for sure but this suggests they have entwined business interests. This might be a strategic stake, so it's worth watching this space for changes in ownership.

Next Steps:

While it is well worth considering the different groups that own a company, there are other factors that are even more important.

data.title: Associated Banc-Corp (NYSE:ASB) Is Increasing Its Dividend To $0.24
data.description:
Associated Banc-Corp (NYSE:ASB) will increase its dividend from last year's comparable payment on the 15th of December to $0.24. This will take the dividend yield to an attractive 3.9%, providing a nice boost to shareholder returns.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Associated Banc-Corp's Payment Expected To Have Solid Earnings Coverage

If the payments aren't sustainable, a high yield for a few years won't matter that much.

Associated Banc-Corp has established itself as a dividend paying company with over 10 years history of distributing earnings to shareholders. Based on Associated Banc-Corp's last earnings report, the payout ratio is at a decent 92%, meaning that the company is able to pay out its dividend with a bit of room to spare.

Looking forward, EPS is forecast to rise by 117.2% over the next 3 years. Despite the current payout ratio being slightly elevated, analysts estimate the future payout ratio will be 32% over the same time period, which would make us comfortable with the sustainability of the dividend.

View our latest analysis for Associated Banc-Corp

Associated Banc-Corp Has A Solid Track Record

The company has been paying a dividend for a long time, and it has been quite stable which gives us confidence in the future dividend potential. Since 2015, the annual payment back then was $0.40, compared to the most recent full-year payment of $0.96. This means that it has been growing its distributions at 9.1% per annum over that time. Dividends have grown at a reasonable rate over this period, and without any major cuts in the payment over time, we think this is an attractive combination as it provides a nice boost to shareholder returns.

Dividend Growth Potential Is Shaky

Some investors will be chomping at the bit to buy some of the company's stock based on its dividend history. However, initial appearances might be deceiving. Earnings per share has been sinking by 12% over the last five years. A sharp decline in earnings per share is not great from from a dividend perspective. Even conservative payout ratios can come under pressure if earnings fall far enough. On the bright side, earnings are predicted to gain some ground over the next year, but until this turns into a pattern we wouldn't be feeling too comfortable.

In Summary

In summary, while it's always good to see the dividend being raised, we don't think Associated Banc-Corp's payments are rock solid. In the past the payments have been stable, but we think the company is paying out too much for this to continue for the long term. Overall, we don't think this company has the makings of a good income stock.

Investors generally tend to favour companies with a consistent, stable dividend policy as opposed to those operating an irregular one. data.title: Evaluating Cintas Shares After National Expansion and a 15% Drop This Year
data.description:
• Ever catch yourself wondering if Cintas stock is a hidden value gem or already priced to perfection? You are not alone, and today we are diving right into that question.
  • Recently, Cintas shares have seen some ups and downs, losing 0.7% over the past week and dropping 15.2% in the last year, but still boasting a strong 113.6% gain over five years.
  • Market chatter has swirled around Cintas after recent news of its ongoing national expansion and strategic partnerships with major healthcare providers. Many believe these developments could shape its long-term outlook. These moves have caught investors' attention and may explain some of the volatility in share price.
  • When it comes to valuation, Cintas currently has a score of 0 out of 6 on our undervaluation checks, so not exactly a slam dunk bargain by traditional metrics. Let us unpack those valuation methods, and stick around, because there is an even smarter way to assess value that you will not want to miss at the end.

Cintas scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Cintas Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the value of a company by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to their present value. This approach helps investors gauge whether a stock’s current price is justified by its expected long-term earnings power.

For Cintas, the latest reported Free Cash Flow sits at $1.69 billion. Analysts forecast the company's annual cash flows will rise over time, with projections reaching approximately $2.60 billion by 2029. Beyond the five-year analyst consensus period, additional growth estimates are extrapolated to provide a full ten-year outlook.

Using this data, the DCF model calculates Cintas’s intrinsic fair value at $161.89 per share. Compared to its current market price, this indicates that Cintas is trading at a 13.6% premium to its estimated fair value. In other words, the stock appears overvalued according to this method, with the market price running ahead of projected cash flows.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Cintas may be overvalued by 13.6%. Discover 905 undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Cintas.

Approach 2: Cintas Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is one of the most widely used valuation tools for profitable companies like Cintas because it directly compares the company's share price to its earnings. This makes it a helpful gauge for judging whether a stock is getting ahead of itself or if there is still value left on the table.

Growth expectations and risk both play a big role in what constitutes a "normal" or fair PE ratio. Typically, companies with higher expected earnings growth or less risk deserve a higher PE, while those facing more uncertainty or slower growth tend to trade at lower multiples.

Cintas currently trades on a PE ratio of 40.06x. For comparison, the industry average PE is 21.95x, and the company's peer group trades at around 30.02x. So, on a pure multiple comparison, Cintas looks expensive.

This is where Simply Wall St’s proprietary "Fair Ratio" comes in. The Fair Ratio for Cintas is 29.94x, derived from factors like the company’s forecast earnings growth, profit margin, scale, and the risks it faces. This provides a more tailored benchmark than just comparing with peers or the wider industry.

Comparing Cintas’ current PE (40.06x) to its Fair Ratio (29.94x), the stock trades well above what would be considered fair based on its fundamentals, suggesting that investors are paying a premium that may not be justified by earnings prospects alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

A Narrative is simply the story you believe about a company: where it is heading, how its industry is changing, and how those changes will affect its financial future. Unlike static models or ratios, Narratives connect the dots between a company’s business outlook, your own assumptions for future revenue, earnings, and margins, and arrive at your view of fair value at the end.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, you will find this tool being used by millions of investors, letting you easily build, share, and compare Narratives. All information is automatically updated when new earnings reports or news headlines break. Narratives help you make buy or sell decisions by transparently comparing your fair value to the current share price, so you can see if your assumptions support action or patience as the company’s story unfolds.
